action: Open Adobe Photoshop if not already open. Create a new document with standard size (e.g., 500x500 pixels). Use the Ellipse Tool to draw a circle centered in the document. Set the color to default black or user preferred if specified.
fallback_action: Perform a Google search using Internet Explorer

recipe.steps: The task is to open Adobe Photoshop (if not already open), create a new 500x500px document, and use the Ellipse Tool to draw a centered black circle. On the current screen, the Adobe Photoshop Elements icon (ID: 564) is visible in the taskbar, but it is not clear if Photoshop is already open. To proceed, I should left-click the Adobe Photoshop Elements icon to open or bring it to the foreground.

The previous action was to left-click the Adobe Photoshop Elements icon to open or bring it to the foreground. Now, the current screen does not show the Photoshop interface yet, but it may be loading or opening. To ensure the application has time to open, I should wait for a moment before proceeding.

After waiting, the screen now shows the Adobe Photoshop Elements interface. The next step is to create a new document with a standard size of 500x500 pixels. Typically, this is done by selecting 'File' > 'New' from the menu. However, the current screen does not clearly show the menu bar or a visible 'New' document button. To proceed, I should look for the 'File' menu or a 'New' document button. Since the interface may have a 'File' menu at the top left, I will click there to look for the 'New' option.

After clicking the 'File' menu, the next logical step is to look for and select the 'New' option to create a new document. However, the current screenshot does not clearly show a dropdown or any new options, possibly because the menu is still loading or the click did not register. To ensure the menu options appear, I should wait briefly to allow the interface to respond.

After waiting, the screenshot still does not show a visible dropdown or new options from the 'File' menu. It is possible that the click did not register or the menu requires another click to display the options. To proceed, I will click the 'File' menu again to try to open the dropdown and access the 'New' document option.

After clicking the 'File' menu again, the screenshot still does not show a visible dropdown or new options from the 'File' menu. This suggests that either the menu is not responding, or the click is not being registered in the correct area. Alternatively, the menu might be appearing elsewhere or the interface may require a different interaction. Since the 'File' menu is a standard way to create a new document, but repeated clicks have not worked, I should look for another way to create a new document. There is an icon labeled 'New' (ID: 101) visible, which is likely a shortcut to create a new document. I will click on the 'New' icon to proceed.

The current screen shows the Photoshop interface, and after clicking the 'New' icon, a new document dialog or options should appear. However, the screenshot does not show a new dialog or any visible change, which may mean the interface is still loading or the click did not register. To ensure the interface has time to respond, I should wait briefly before taking further action.

After waiting, the screen still shows the main Photoshop Elements interface, and there is no visible new document dialog or options. The previous click on the 'New' icon may not have registered, or the interface may require a double-click or another interaction. Since the 'New' icon is the most likely way to create a new document and single-clicking did not work, I will try double-clicking the 'New' icon to see if that opens the new document dialog.

After double-clicking the 'New' icon, the screen now shows a document with the title 'Untitled-1 @ 100% (Quick Mask/8)' and a visible canvas area. This suggests that a new document has been created. However, the document size is shown as '1743 pX x 294 px (300 ppi)' in the lower bar, which is not the required 500x500 pixels. To create a new document with the correct size, I need to find the option to specify custom dimensions. The 'File' menu is a standard place to start this process, so I will try clicking the 'File' menu again to look for a 'New' or 'New Document' option that allows setting the size.

After clicking the 'File' menu (ID: 56), the menu bar at the top of the screen is now open, showing options such as 'Open', 'Browse in Bridge', 'Open as', 'Open as Smart Object', 'Open Recent', 'Close', 'Close All', 'Close Others', 'Close and Go to Bridge', 'Save', 'Save As', 'Save a Copy', 'Revert', 'Export', 'Generate', 'Share', 'Search Adobe Stock', 'Place Embedded', 'Place Linked', 'Package', 'File Info', 'Print', 'Print One', 'Scripts', 'Automate', 'Import', and 'Export'. The 'New' option (with a document icon and shortcut Ctrl+N) is visible (ID: 53). To create a new document with custom dimensions, I should click the 'New' option.

After clicking 'New' from the File menu, the screen now shows the new document dialog. The current preset is 'Custom' with dimensions 1743 x 294 px. To create a 500x500 px document, I need to change the width and height fields. The width field (ID: 53) is currently set to 1743. I should click on the width field to edit it to 500.

The new document dialog is open, and the width field is currently selected (showing 1743). To set the document size to 500x500 pixels, I need to type '500' in the width field first. This will ensure the width is set correctly before moving on to the height field.

The width field has been set to 500. Now, to set the document to 500x500 pixels, I need to change the height field as well, which is currently set to 294. The height field is located next to the width field. The next step is to click on the height field to select it for editing.

The height field is now selected, and it currently displays '294'. To set the document size to 500x500 pixels, I need to type '500' in the height field, just as I did for the width field. This will ensure both dimensions are set correctly before proceeding to create the document.

Both the width and height fields are now set to 500, so the document will be 500x500 pixels. The next step is to confirm and create the new document. Typically, there is a 'Create' or 'OK' button in the new document dialog. I see a button labeled 'Create' (ID: 100) in the lower right of the dialog. Clicking this will create the new document with the specified dimensions.

The new document has been created with the correct size (500x500 pixels). The next step is to use the Ellipse Tool to draw a centered black circle. The current screen shows the Photoshop workspace, but the Ellipse Tool is not yet selected. Typically, the Ellipse Tool is found in the toolbar on the left side of the screen. I should look for the Ellipse Tool icon and click it to select the tool before drawing the circle.

The Ellipse Tool has now been selected, as indicated by the previous action and the current screen. The next step is to draw a centered black circle on the 500x500 pixel canvas. To do this, I need to click and drag from the center of the canvas while holding Shift+Alt (to constrain proportions and draw from the center), but since keyboard shortcuts are not allowed, I will approximate by clicking near the center of the canvas and dragging outward to create a circle. The center of the canvas is likely around the middle of the visible white area. I will start by clicking near the center of the canvas to begin drawing the ellipse.

The Ellipse Tool is selected, and the last action was a left-click near the center of the canvas to start drawing the circle. To complete the circle, I need to drag outward from the center to define the size of the ellipse. Since only one action is allowed at a time, the next logical step is to perform another left-click (or click-and-drag) outward from the center to create the circle. However, since click-and-drag is not an available action, I will left-click again at a point away from the center to simulate the end point of the ellipse, which should create the circle shape.

The Ellipse Tool has been used to draw a shape on the canvas, likely creating an ellipse or circle. The next step is to ensure the shape is filled with the default black color, as required by the task. The current screen does not clearly show the color settings or the properties panel. To proceed, I should look for the color picker or properties panel, which is often located on the right side of the Photoshop interface. Since there is an icon labeled 'Ps' (ID: 11) which may represent the properties or color panel, I will click on it to check if it brings up the color options.

After clicking the icon that might represent the properties or color panel, the right side of the screen now shows the 'Properties' panel, which includes options for 'Fill' and 'Stroke'. The 'Fill' icon (ID: 47) is visible, and it is currently set to white. To set the circle to black, I need to change the fill color to black. The next step is to click on the 'Fill' icon to open the color picker and select black.

After clicking the 'Fill' icon, a color picker dialog is now visible. To set the fill color to black, I need to select the black color from the color picker. The black color is typically located at the bottom left of the color picker area. I will click on the black color swatch to set the fill color to black.
